---
title: |
  Machine Learning for Characterization and Processing<br>
  Unit 1: Why Materials Signals Challenge ML
bibliography: ref.bib
author:
  - name: Prof. Dr. Philipp Pelz
    affiliation:
      - FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg
execute:
  eval: false
format:
  revealjs:
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    css: custom.css
    theme: custom.scss
    slide-number: c/t
    logo: "eclipse_logo_small.png"
    footer: "© Philipp Pelz - ML for Characterization and Processing"
---

## Unit 1 goals

By the end of today, you can:

1. Classify key data modalities in characterization and processing.
2. Explain why naive ML fails on experimental materials data.
3. Use PSPP (processing-structure-property-performance) as modeling scaffold.
4. Build a first trustworthy baseline workflow.

## Course positioning

- MFML gives the mathematical core.
- This course focuses on *messy experimental reality*:
  - micrographs
  - spectra
  - process logs
  - multimodal data fusion

## What makes materials signals difficult?

- heteroscedastic noise
- instrument drift and calibration artifacts
- domain shift across labs and instruments
- sparse labels and expensive annotation

::: aside
@neuer2024machine; @ryan2021machine
:::

## PSPP graph as ML problem map

- Processing $\rightarrow$ Structure
- Structure $\rightarrow$ Property
- Property $\rightarrow$ Performance

ML tasks can target each edge, but physics constraints matter at every step.

## Minimal trustworthy pipeline

1. define deployment question
2. choose leakage-safe split
3. train simple baseline
4. evaluate with task-relevant metrics
5. perform failure analysis + uncertainty check

## Common anti-patterns (avoid)

- random split across correlated samples
- metric-only reporting without error modes
- no uncertainty estimates for high-stakes decisions

## Unit 1 case sketch

Example: porosity detection in additive manufacturing

- inputs: image + process log
- output: defect risk class
- pitfalls: class imbalance, shift between machines, annotation noise

## Exercise handoff (90 min)

- train baseline model on a small dataset
- compare random vs grouped split
- inspect confusion matrix / MAE
- propose one physics-aware feature

## Unit 1 takeaways

- Strong pipelines beat fancy models on weak data.
- Evaluation design is part of the model.
- Physics-aware reasoning is non-optional in this domain.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
